sentences.text: No, they let the girls go.
And we have to go back to the barracks, you know?
We go back to the barrack.
This was in Auschwitz.
And the men, they sent them to Gross-Rosen.
He went and took them from Gross-Rosen.
He didn't know It was 1,000 men-- 300 women.
But he got the factory before Schindler.
I didn't know Schindler before.
He got a lot of Jewish he works for him.
And he was very close with the Jewish people.
Schindler, by himself.
He came.
Everybody from the women on the list, they go to the train.
Yeah.
My God-- he was such a nice man.
Too bad he passed away so young-- too bad.
What he for the Jewish people in the world-- nobody.
And he was an SS.
I think the SS was, you know, covered.
He made big business.
Yeah.
And everybody went to Brinnlitz.
Yeah.
Special train, sure.
And the men, they waited, they stayed, they looked over everybody that's here.
But we went.
Yeah, they got a list.
They know they have-- they have to let us out from Auschwitz.
Otherwise, it wasn't so easy to go from Auschwitz.
Or you go northern, or something else.
We came to Brinnlitz.
They saw us-- how we dressed.
Nothing-- we didn't go-- we didn't have nothing.
Yeah, in-- in Auschwitz, when we stayed from the morning-- 4 o'clock to 7:00-- an appell.
Appell is they count everybody is in the-- in the barracks.
They stay outside.
It was so so cold.
Nothing underneath-- just one dress and-- and the [NON-ENGLISH] shoes.
Nothing more.
It was so cold.
Yeah.
The winter was so bad.
It was like now here.
And when we stayed in the appell, the one girl came to me.
And she says, I know you.
The same town-- a small town.
And she was in one barrack.
She was a supervisor.
She got everything.
No.
So many years-- no.
I don't-- but I-- but I know who she is.
And this was about three weeks.
We were in Auschwitz about-- we slept up [NON-ENGLISH], like from here-- this table-- this table, there was 14 people.
That 14 people-- this one pritch.
Was so-- you couldn't move.
A bed.
They called it a pritch.
It's 14 people.
How can you live like this?
And-- and then when we came to Brinnlitz, we didn't get anything.
I was sewing by hand, collars for the men's shirts.
Sometimes they ripped, and by hand, I was sewing.
And over there, they give me a piece of bread, you know.
And then I got a one bread-- a big bread.
It was 2-- 2 kilo-- 2 pounds-- kilo?
Pound?
Yeah.
It was big in Poland-- they make the bread like this.
I-- every day I switched fresh-- and they gave me the fresh and I give them this.
And I got for my husband, you know, and for myself.
No.
In a pritch-- in another barrack.
In Brinnlitz.
Sure.
Where can you live?
They can't give you a-- a room.
No.
It was lucky.
But underneath was one woman.
She was a other man's wife-- no, mother.
And then was a factory in Brinnlitz from the wool.
This factory got a lot of wool.
And the factory didn't exist anymore.
The people, they left.
The men, they steal the wool in the night, and they bring to the women-- and they make sweaters.
They make skirts.
The one lady, she makes me a skirt and a jacket.
I got for to wear, you know.
And I gave them something.
I don't remember whether bread or something.
And I got, every day, something to do.
I-- I was sewing-- after the work, we came to the barrack-- and I was sewing, you know, by hand.
And I got some things to eat.
And then, by-- by-- Schindler-- was-- the men, they worked on the big machines.
They-- they-- they were for the military, you know-- I don't know how you call it.
The factory-- they make everything-- of--
Ammunition factory.
Yeah, ammunition factory.
The women, they stayed and they work, you know.
They didn't got work to do.
They stayed.
And-- we did some things.
What you can do?
Until your dead, you know?
And Schindler was supposed to become a commission-- became commissions off.
All the woman has to do-- has to be here, and everybody has to work.
And then Leipholtz, the Dutch, the-- the-- the German, the SS, he saw us.
He says to Schindler, they don't have nothing to do.
What do they do here?
They stay?
Nothing?
What they did-- he says, no.
It's not going to be like this.
I will take all the women in the woods, and I'll kill them.
Yes.
Yes.
He wants the kill us.
Everybody.
And Schindler heard this.
He says to himself-- he was very close with the Jewish people.
Very close, he told them-- this and this.
So he says he has to go someplace.
He went to the German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the front-- you know, the-- the-- military front.
He says, he has to do something to him, because it's no good that he is by us here.
He went to the German.
He took the-- the-- he went, he says, this and this-- sent in papers that Leipholtz has to go in front where they fight.
He came back in about a week or two.
He has papers.
He has to go in front, where they fight.
And this was for us.
We were saved.
Otherwise, he would do this.
He would kill everybody.
This was Schindler-- what he did to us.
Otherwise, nobody would be alive from the women.
He saved us.
It was a very big factory.
Then one day, they bring a train with people-- so frozen and everything.
The people, they fell from the train if they opened the door.
And like-- like-- Muselmann.
Muselmann was so skin and bones-- nothing else.
Nobody wants to take them.
It was 100 people, I think-- 100 people.
He makes barracks.
He says, I take them.
Yeah.
His wife was, too, over there.
He made the barracks for these people.
A lot of people was frozen to death, you know.
She cooked for them, farina.
You know what farina is?
And she got as a small spoon-- she feed them like babies-- like babies.
A lot of people survived from these people.
And this was a few months.
Who knows?
Nobody let them in.
Nobody.
He says, I take them.
And he took them.
A lot of people survived.
No.
Yeah.
We saw them.
And she did-- Schindler's wife.
How can you go?
You have to work in your place.
For Schindler?
We stayed and worked.
We didn't got nothing to do.
Ammunition.
Yeah.
Yeah-- everybody worked in the ammunition factory.
Everybody.
He left with these seven people.
To-- I don't know-- Australia, someplace.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was the end.
Now, before the-- it was the two German police.
We walk a lot over there-- and they look of you, too.
There was one police-- German police-- he watched you.
And you got, every day, a piece of bread, like this.
One was so terrible, he cut the head off, and took this bread-- and let them-- and let them-- and they [INAUDIBLE], where they went-- We went from-- from town to town.
And came to-- when we was in Brinnlitz, and we hear the war is almost finished.
So we take the two guys-- and the people, they kept one guy, they don't let them go.
They stay, and they look like in prison-- you know?
And Schindler says, the night before, this and this, the front is very close.
The war is going to end.
If you could see what's going on over there-- my God.
Everybody was crying.
And everybody was happy and crying.
They don't-- we don't have nobody.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Before he tell us, he got-- before he tell us that the war is over, but he got birthday about a month before.
You should have seen what we did for him.
Everybody throw him in the-- in the air.
And everybody was so happy, you know?
And we make those-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everybody-- we got everybody's name in an album, wrote in Polish.
Everybody's name he got, from all the people he saved.
And there was a big kitchen.
In the kitchen was big men, you know?
And the German looked, and they says, what you think?
The Russian coming?
Don't wait for them.
They're not coming here.
We are here.
Then Schindler told us that the war is over.
He left in the night with these seven Jewish people.
He took the car, the wife, something else-- what he has to take.
He left.
Yeah, they worked with us together.
The name-- I remember one name-- Stern.
I don't remember any more.
Seven people got-- he was very close with them in the Emalia factory.
Very close.
They worked for-- for him, you know.
I don't know who this is.
I don't think--
For Schindler?
I don't know.
I know they were looking for him.
All the Germans, they left what they was here.
I have no idea.
I know they looking for him.
If they would be catch him, he would be killed.
But he was so smart.
He left with the Jewish people and he survived.
To who?
To me?
Everybody goes where they want to go.
I was crazy-- I go to Poland.
And I was-- and I says to my husband, maybe somebody is in Poland.
Maybe it's not true.
Who knows?
And I stayed in Poland.
The last one, they let us out-- So what happened?
So do--
When we came to Poland--
By train.
It was over there-- a factory from-- a textile factory.
Everybody got material for coats, you know, or something else.
This is what they give you.
We went to a-- a Jewish gymnasium.
And the Jewish people had a big beautiful building.
So we lived over there.
We got one room, and lived over there.
Yeah, yeah.
And then my brother was-- came.
Sigmund.
The other was killed.
He came and was over there, too.
He came.
Then in a few days there was a pogrom.
The Russian makes a pogrom  in military-- yeah-- In Krakow.
Then--
Oh yeah, in that program, a lot of people was killed.
What they did?
They came.
I don't know.
You know-- they did the things they don't have to do with the Jewish people-- it was Jewish people.
I saw what they did.
They-- they-- they killed some people.
It was a pogrom for the Jewish people, you know-- the pogrom.
They take you out from the [NON-ENGLISH].
They go-- yeah-- they go inside.
What they can-- they took it.
You know?
The Russians, they took it.
But we were lucky we were alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This was in Krakow.
No.
This I don't know.
They killed somebody, but I don't know who.
There was so many people, you know-- it was a very very big building.
And this building, they got the rooms-- who can-- they stay in-- I didn't got money.
And Berel didn't got money.
And they didn't give you money from the Jewish committee.
They didn't give you nothing.
I don't know how-- it was a long time.
Then from Krakow, my husband, he got some Polack-- he used to come to him.
He put the money, what he got, something he sells-- that he bring him from time to time, you know.
And you didn't got nothing.
You got nothing to start with something.
After this, we were in Poland a long time.
Was-- we got somebody to tell us to go to [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
Over there there's a big factory.
He started to work, I started to work in the factory.
It was a very, very big factory-- 5,000 people.
But the factory wasn't in one place.
It was in different places-- small towns and big towns.
I was together and my husband used to work for them.
And he was so good-- the-- all the-- the presidents from the factory, they came, and they asked him what to do.
You know?
That's why he came here.
He didn't work so hard his whole life like he worked here.
